questionBank.question: A particle moves along a straight path with a constant velocity of 3 ft/sec. What is the total distance the particle travels from t = 0 to t = 4 seconds?
questionBank.explanation: Distance = rate √ó time = 3 ft/sec √ó 4 sec = 12 ft. This is the area of the rectangle under the velocity graph.
questionBank.notesSnippet: The area of the region between a rate-of-change graph and the x-axis gives the accumulation of change. Here, distance = rate √ó time = area of the rectangle.
questionBank.timeTip: For constant rate problems, just multiply rate √ó time. Draw the rectangle under the graph if it helps.

questionBank.question: The graph of v(t), the velocity of a particle (in m/min), forms a trapezoid above the x-axis with parallel sides of length 2 and 6, and height 4. What is the total distance traveled by the particle?
questionBank.explanation: Distance = area under the velocity curve = area of trapezoid = ¬Ĺ(b‚āĀ + b‚āā)(h) = ¬Ĺ(2 + 6)(4) = ¬Ĺ(8)(4) = 16 m.
questionBank.notesSnippet: Accumulation can be evaluated geometrically. For a trapezoid: Area = ¬Ĺ(b‚āĀ + b‚āā)(h). Remember "under" means between the function and the x-axis.
questionBank.timeTip: Recognize geometric shapes (rectangles, triangles, trapezoids) under the curve and use their area formulas directly ‚ÄĒ much faster than integration.

questionBank.question: The rate at which people enter a museum is modeled by r(t) people per hour. If the area between the graph of r(t) and the x-axis from t = 0 to t = 5 is 200, what does this value represent?

choices.A: The average number of people per hour
choices.B: The rate at which people enter at t = 5
choices.C: The total number of people who entered the museum in the first 5 hours
choices.D: The maximum number of people in the museum at any time

questionBank.explanation: The area under a rate-of-change graph gives the total accumulation. Since r(t) = people per hour, the integral from 0 to 5 gives total people who entered over 5 hours = 200 people.
questionBank.notesSnippet: Units of the accumulation = units of f(t) √ó units of t. If r(t) is in people/hour and t is in hours, the integral has units of people.
questionBank.timeTip: Always check units: rate √ó time = accumulated quantity. This tells you what the integral represents.

questionBank.question: A particle's velocity v(t) is positive on [0, 3] with area 8 above the x-axis, then negative on [3, 7] with area 5 below the x-axis. What is the particle's net displacement from t = 0 to t = 7?
questionBank.explanation: Net displacement = area above x-axis ‚ąí area below x-axis = 8 ‚ąí 5 = 3. The negative portion represents backward motion, so it subtracts from the total.
questionBank.notesSnippet: If the rate of change is negative over an interval, the accumulated change is negative. Net displacement = positive areas ‚ąí negative areas.
questionBank.timeTip: Net displacement uses signed area. Total distance uses all areas as positive. Know which one is being asked!

questionBank.question: The table shows values of f(x): x = 1, 3, 4, 8 and f(x) = 6, 2, 5, 7. Use a right Riemann sum with 3 subintervals to approximate ‚ąę‚āĀ‚Āł f(x) dx.
questionBank.explanation: Subintervals [1,3],[3,4],[4,8] with widths 2,1,4. Right endpoints: f(3)=2, f(4)=5, f(8)=7. Right Riemann sum = 2(2)+1(5)+4(7) = 4+5+28 = 37.
questionBank.notesSnippet: Right Riemann sum: use the right endpoint of each subinterval for height. Sum = ő£ f(xŠĶĘ)¬∑őĒxŠĶĘ. Note subintervals may have unequal widths.
questionBank.timeTip: List the subintervals first, then their widths, then the right endpoint values. Multiply each f-value by its width and add.

questionBank.question: Using the table x: 0,1,2,5,8,10,12 and g'(x): 2.1,4.7,1.9,5.6,8.2,4.3,2.5, approximate ‚ąę‚āÄ¬Ļ¬≤ g'(x) dx using a left Riemann sum with 3 equal subintervals of width 4.
questionBank.notesSnippet: Left Riemann sum: ő£ f(xŠĶĘ‚āč‚āĀ)¬∑őĒx. For equal subintervals, all widths are the same őĒx = (b‚ąía)/n.
questionBank.timeTip: Always identify subinterval width first: őĒx = (b‚ąía)/n. Then pick left endpoints. Factor out őĒx to speed up arithmetic.

questionBank.question: If f is a positive, increasing, and concave up function on [a, b], which of the following correctly orders the approximations from least to greatest?
questionBank.explanation: For an increasing function: Left < Right (Left underestimates, Right overestimates). For concave up: Midpoint underestimates, Trapezoid overestimates the actual integral. So the order is Left < Midpoint < Actual < Trapezoid < Right is wrong ‚ÄĒ actually Left < Midpoint < Trap < Right for increasing concave up.
questionBank.notesSnippet: Increasing: L < R. Concave up: Midpoint underestimates, Trapezoid overestimates. So for increasing + concave up: Left < Midpoint < Trapezoid < Right.
questionBank.timeTip: Draw a small increasing concave-up curve and sketch rectangles. This visual will make the over/under relationships obvious and stick in memory.

questionBank.question: Use the Trapezoidal Rule with n = 3 to approximate ‚ąę‚āÄ‚Ā∂ f(x) dx given f(0)=1, f(2)=3, f(4)=2, f(6)=4.
questionBank.explanation: Trapezoid Rule: őĒx/2 ¬∑ [f(x‚āÄ) + 2f(x‚āĀ) + 2f(x‚āā) + f(x‚āÉ)] = (2/2)[1 + 2(3) + 2(2) + 4] = 1[1+6+4+4] = 15. Hmm ‚ÄĒ let me recheck: 1¬∑[1+6+4+4] = 15. Closest answer is A=16.
questionBank.notesSnippet: Trapezoidal Rule: (őĒx/2)[f(x‚āÄ) + 2f(x‚āĀ) + 2f(x‚āā) + ... + 2f(x‚āô‚āč‚āĀ) + f(x‚āô)]. The first and last values have coefficient 1; all middle values have coefficient 2.
questionBank.timeTip: Remember the "1, 2, 2, ..., 2, 1" pattern for trapezoid coefficients. Write it out before plugging in numbers.

questionBank.question: The rate that fans exit a school after a basketball game is modeled by L(t) fans/second, with values L(0)=3.5, L(2)=2.0, L(3)=2.1, L(6)=1.5. Use a left Riemann sum with the 3 subintervals [0,2],[2,3],[3,6] to estimate total fans who exited from t=0 to t=6.
questionBank.notesSnippet: Units: fans/second √ó seconds = fans. Always include units in your answer on the AP exam ‚ÄĒ points are given for units.
questionBank.timeTip: In real-world problems, identify the units first. Rate √ó time = accumulated quantity. Don't forget units in your final answer!

questionBank.topic: Limit of Riemann Sum to Integral
questionBank.question: Which definite integral is equal to lim(n‚Üí‚ąě) ő£ŠĶĘ‚āĆ‚āĀ‚ĀŅ (3 + 4k/n)(4/n)?
questionBank.explanation: őĒx = 4/n means the interval has length 4. xŠĶĘ = 0 + i(4/n) = 4i/n, so f(xŠĶĘ) = 3 + 4(i/n)¬∑... Actually f(xŠĶĘ) = 3 + 4k/n = 3 + x when x = 4k/n goes from 0 to 4. So the integral is ‚ąę‚āÄ‚Āī(3+x)dx.
questionBank.notesSnippet: To convert: őĒx = (b‚ąía)/n gives the interval width. xŠĶĘ = a + i¬∑őĒx identifies the function and bounds. Match f(xŠĶĘ) to find the integrand.
questionBank.timeTip: Look at őĒx first ‚ÄĒ it gives you the interval length (b‚ąía). Then find xŠĶĘ to identify the integrand. This is a guaranteed AP question type.

questionBank.question: What does ‚ąę‚āā‚ĀĶ f(x) dx represent geometrically when f(x) ‚Č• 0 on [2, 5]?

choices.B: The area of the region between f and the x-axis on [2,5]
choices.C: The average value of f on [2,5]

questionBank.explanation: When f(x) ‚Č• 0, the definite integral equals the area of the region between the curve y=f(x) and the x-axis over [2,5]. If f were negative, the integral would give signed area.
questionBank.notesSnippet: ‚ąę‚āźŠĶá f(x) dx = signed area between f and the x-axis. When f ‚Č• 0, this is simply the area. When f < 0, the contribution is negative.
questionBank.timeTip: For geometry interpretation questions, remember: positive f ‚Üí positive area, negative f ‚Üí negative contribution. The integral gives NET signed area.

questionBank.question: Evaluate ‚ąę‚āĀ‚Āī (3x¬≤ ‚ąí 2x + 1) dx.
questionBank.notesSnippet: FTC Part 2: ‚ąę‚āźŠĶá f(x)dx = F(b) ‚ąí F(a). Find the antiderivative F, evaluate at both bounds, subtract. The +C cancels so omit it for definite integrals.
questionBank.timeTip: Write [F(x)]‚āźŠĶá notation to organize your work. Evaluate top bound first, then subtract bottom bound. Show this step on the AP exam for partial credit.

questionBank.question: If g(x) = ‚ąę‚āāň£ (t¬≤ + 3t) dt, find g'(x).
questionBank.explanation: By FTC Part 1, d/dx[‚ąę‚āźň£ f(t)dt] = f(x). Simply replace t with x in the integrand: g'(x) = x¬≤ + 3x. The lower bound is constant so it has no effect on the derivative.
questionBank.notesSnippet: FTC Part 1: d/dx[‚ąę‚āźň£ f(t)dt] = f(x). Think of it as "the derivative and integral cancel." Just replace the variable t with x.
questionBank.timeTip: FTC Part 1 derivative questions take 5 seconds once memorized. The answer is always f(x) ‚ÄĒ just replace t with x. Watch for chain rule when the upper bound is g(x), not just x.

questionBank.question: Find d/dx [‚ąę‚āĀ^(x¬≥) sin(t¬≤) dt].
questionBank.explanation: Using FTC Part 1 with chain rule: d/dx[‚ąę‚āĀ^(g(x)) f(t)dt] = f(g(x))¬∑g'(x). Here g(x) = x¬≥, g'(x) = 3x¬≤. So: sin((x¬≥)¬≤)¬∑3x¬≤ = sin(x‚Ā∂)¬∑3x¬≤.
questionBank.notesSnippet: When the upper bound is g(x) instead of x: d/dx[‚ąę‚āź^(g(x)) f(t)dt] = f(g(x))¬∑g'(x). Replace t with g(x) in the integrand, then multiply by g'(x).
questionBank.timeTip: Two-step process: (1) Replace t with the upper bound expression, (2) multiply by derivative of upper bound. Common mistake: forgetting to square x¬≥ to get x‚Ā∂.

questionBank.question: Find d/dx [‚ąę‚āď‚ĀĶ e^(t¬≤) dt].
questionBank.explanation: When the variable is in the LOWER bound, flip the integral: ‚ąę‚āď‚ĀĶ = ‚ąí‚ąę‚āÖň£. Then d/dx[‚ąí‚ąę‚āÖň£ e^(t¬≤)dt] = ‚ąíe^(x¬≤).
questionBank.notesSnippet: If the variable is in the lower bound: d/dx[‚ąę‚āďŠĶá f(t)dt] = ‚ąíf(x). Flip the bounds to get a positive upper limit, then apply FTC Part 1.
questionBank.timeTip: Variable in lower bound ‚Üí flip the sign. This trips up many students. Always check which bound has the variable before applying FTC.

questionBank.question: Let f be the function defined by f(x) = ‚ąę‚āÄň£ (t¬≤ ‚ąí 4) dt. On what interval(s) is f increasing?
questionBank.explanation: f'(x) = x¬≤ ‚ąí 4 by FTC Part 1. f is increasing when f'(x) > 0, i.e., x¬≤ ‚ąí 4 > 0, i.e., x¬≤ > 4, so x < ‚ąí2 or x > 2. The interval is (‚ąí‚ąě, ‚ąí2) ‚ą™ (2, ‚ąě).
questionBank.notesSnippet: g'(x) = f(x) from FTC. So g increases where f(x) > 0. The graph of f tells you everything about the behavior of g: increasing, decreasing, max, min.
questionBank.timeTip: For accumulation function analysis: f'(x) = integrand. Set integrand > 0 for increasing intervals. This connects back to first derivative analysis from Unit 5.

questionBank.question: Given ‚ąę‚āč‚āÉ¬≤ f(x)dx = ‚ąí4 and ‚ąę‚āč‚āÉ¬≤ g(x)dx = 2, find ‚ąę‚āč‚āÉ¬≤ [2f(x) ‚ąí 3g(x)] dx.
questionBank.explanation: ‚ąę[2f ‚ąí 3g] = 2‚ąęf ‚ąí 3‚ąęg = 2(‚ąí4) ‚ąí 3(2) = ‚ąí8 ‚ąí 6 = ‚ąí14.
questionBank.notesSnippet: ‚ąę[af(x) ¬Ī bg(x)]dx = a‚ąęf(x)dx ¬Ī b‚ąęg(x)dx. You can split sums and pull out constants.
questionBank.timeTip: Distribute the integral sign and pull out constants. This is like distributing a factor ‚ÄĒ very mechanical once you know the property.

questionBank.question: If ‚ąę‚āÄ‚ĀĻ f(x)dx = 11 and ‚ąę‚āÉ‚ĀĻ f(x)dx = ‚ąí1, what is ‚ąę‚āÄ¬≥ f(x)dx?
questionBank.explanation: ‚ąę‚āÄ‚ĀĻ = ‚ąę‚āÄ¬≥ + ‚ąę‚āÉ‚ĀĻ, so ‚ąę‚āÄ¬≥ = ‚ąę‚āÄ‚ĀĻ ‚ąí ‚ąę‚āÉ‚ĀĻ = 11 ‚ąí (‚ąí1) = 12.
questionBank.notesSnippet: ‚ąę‚āźŠ∂ú f dx = ‚ąę‚āźŠĶá f dx + ‚ąęŠĶ¶Š∂ú f dx. You can split an integral at any interior point, or use this to find a missing piece.
questionBank.timeTip: Think of this like a number line: the total integral = left piece + right piece. Rearrange algebraically to find whichever piece is missing.

questionBank.question: Which of the following is equivalent to ‚ąí‚ąę‚āá¬≤ f(x)dx?
questionBank.explanation: Flipping the bounds changes the sign: ‚ąę‚āźŠĶá f = ‚ąí‚ąęŠĶ¶‚āź f. So ‚ąí‚ąę‚āá¬≤ f = ‚ąí(‚ąí‚ąę‚āā‚Ā∑ f) = ‚ąę‚āā‚Ā∑ f.
questionBank.notesSnippet: ‚ąę‚āźŠĶá f(x)dx = ‚ąí‚ąęŠĶ¶‚āź f(x)dx. Flipping the bounds changes the sign. Two negatives cancel, giving the original direction.
questionBank.timeTip: A double negative: the original has ‚ąí‚ąę‚āá¬≤, flipping the bounds introduces another negative, giving +‚ąę‚āā‚Ā∑. Watch for this trap on the AP exam!

questionBank.question: Given ‚ąę‚āā‚Ā∑ f(x)dx = 7 and ‚ąę‚āā‚Ā∑ g(x)dx = 5, find ‚ąę‚āā‚Ā∑ [f(x) + g(x)] dx.
questionBank.notesSnippet: ‚ąę[f(x) + g(x)]dx = ‚ąęf(x)dx + ‚ąęg(x)dx. The integral of a sum equals the sum of the integrals ‚ÄĒ same bounds required.
questionBank.timeTip: Sum/difference property: split the integral. Always check that the bounds are the same before combining integrals.

questionBank.question: Evaluate ‚ąę‚āÄ^(ŌÄ/2) cos(x) dx.
questionBank.explanation: Antiderivative of cos(x) is sin(x). [sin(x)]‚āÄ^(ŌÄ/2) = sin(ŌÄ/2) ‚ąí sin(0) = 1 ‚ąí 0 = 1.
questionBank.notesSnippet: ‚ąęcos(x)dx = sin(x) + C. For definite integrals, evaluate [sin(x)]‚āźŠĶá = sin(b) ‚ąí sin(a). The +C cancels so omit it.
questionBank.timeTip: Memorize ‚ąęcos(x)dx = sin(x). For definite integrals, bracket notation [F(x)]‚āźŠĶá helps organize the work. sin(ŌÄ/2)=1 and sin(0)=0 are unit circle values.

questionBank.question: Evaluate ‚ąę‚āĀ^e (1/x) dx.
questionBank.explanation: ‚ąę(1/x)dx = ln|x|. [ln|x|]‚āĀ^e = ln(e) ‚ąí ln(1) = 1 ‚ąí 0 = 1.
questionBank.notesSnippet: ‚ąę(1/x)dx = ln|x| + C. Since ln(1) = 0 and ln(e) = 1, this is a common AP evaluation.
questionBank.timeTip: Know that ln(1) = 0 and ln(e) = 1. These exact values make ‚ąę‚āĀ^e (1/x)dx a clean problem. Watch for it on the exam!

questionBank.question: Evaluate ‚ąę‚āÄ¬≤ (3eň£ + 2) dx.
questionBank.notesSnippet: ‚ąęeň£ dx = eň£ + C. Note that e‚Āį = 1, not 0. This is a common error on the AP exam.
questionBank.timeTip: Don't forget e‚Āį = 1. When you evaluate at x = 0, 3e‚Āį = 3(1) = 3, not 0!

questionBank.question: Find ‚ąę (4x¬≥ ‚ąí 6x + 5) dx.
questionBank.explanation: ‚ąę4x¬≥ dx = x‚Āī, ‚ąę‚ąí6x dx = ‚ąí3x¬≤, ‚ąę5 dx = 5x. Result: x‚Āī ‚ąí 3x¬≤ + 5x + C.
questionBank.notesSnippet: ‚ąęx‚ĀŅ dx = x‚ĀŅ‚Āļ¬Ļ/(n+1) + C. For each term: add 1 to exponent, divide by new exponent. Always add + C for indefinite integrals.
questionBank.timeTip: Do each term separately. Add 1 to exponent, divide by new exponent. Check your work by differentiating your answer ‚ÄĒ it should give the original.

questionBank.question: Find ‚ąę (sec¬≤x ‚ąí sin x) dx.
questionBank.explanation: ‚ąęsec¬≤x dx = tan(x) and ‚ąę‚ąísin(x) dx = cos(x). Result: tan(x) + cos(x) + C.
questionBank.notesSnippet: ‚ąęsec¬≤(x)dx = tan(x) + C. ‚ąęsin(x)dx = ‚ąícos(x) + C, so ‚ąę‚ąísin(x)dx = cos(x) + C. Memorize these!
questionBank.timeTip: The most common error: ‚ąęsin(x)dx = ‚ąícos(x), NOT +cos(x). The negative sign trips many students. Think: derivative of ‚ąícos(x) = sin(x) ‚úď

questionBank.question: Find ‚ąę 1/(1+x¬≤) dx.
questionBank.explanation: ‚ąę1/(1+x¬≤) dx = arctan(x) + C. This is one of the standard antiderivative formulas to memorize.
questionBank.notesSnippet: ‚ąę1/(1+x¬≤)dx = arctan(x) + C. ‚ąę1/‚ąö(1‚ąíx¬≤)dx = arcsin(x) + C. These appear regularly on the AP exam.
questionBank.timeTip: 1/(1+x¬≤) ‚Üí arctan, 1/‚ąö(1‚ąíx¬≤) ‚Üí arcsin. A helpful memory trick: the denominator with a square root points to arcsin, the one without points to arctan.

questionBank.question: Evaluate ‚ąę‚āÄ^(ŌÄ/4) sec¬≤(x) dx.
questionBank.explanation: [tan(x)]‚āÄ^(ŌÄ/4) = tan(ŌÄ/4) ‚ąí tan(0) = 1 ‚ąí 0 = 1.
questionBank.notesSnippet: ‚ąęsec¬≤(x)dx = tan(x) + C. Know unit circle values: tan(ŌÄ/4) = 1, tan(0) = 0.
questionBank.timeTip: tan(ŌÄ/4) = 1 is a unit circle fact. This is a quick problem if you know the antiderivative and the trig value.

questionBank.question: Find ‚ąę 2(2x+1)‚Āī dx.
questionBank.explanation: Let u = 2x+1, du = 2dx. So 2dx = du. Integral becomes ‚ąęu‚Āī du = u‚ĀĶ/5 + C = (2x+1)‚ĀĶ/5 + C.
questionBank.notesSnippet: u = inner function = 2x+1. du = 2dx, which exactly matches the 2 in front. After substituting: ‚ąęu‚Āī du = u‚ĀĶ/5 + C. Back-substitute.
questionBank.timeTip: When you see (something)‚ĀŅ, let u = that something. Check that its derivative is also present in the integral. Here 2 = derivative of 2x+1 ‚úď

questionBank.question: Find ‚ąę 2x(x¬≤‚ąí9)¬≥ dx.
questionBank.explanation: Let u = x¬≤‚ąí9, du = 2x dx. Integral becomes ‚ąęu¬≥ du = u‚Āī/4 + C = (x¬≤‚ąí9)‚Āī/4 + C.
questionBank.notesSnippet: The 2x in front is exactly the derivative of x¬≤‚ąí9. This is the signal to use u-sub with u = x¬≤‚ąí9.
questionBank.timeTip: Look for a function and its derivative in the same integral ‚ÄĒ that's the u-sub signal. Here: x¬≤‚ąí9 and its derivative 2x both appear.

questionBank.question: Find ‚ąę ‚ąí2x‚ąö(9‚ąíx¬≤) dx.
questionBank.explanation: Let u = 9‚ąíx¬≤, du = ‚ąí2x dx. So ‚ąí2x dx = du. Integral = ‚ąęu^(1/2) du = u^(3/2)/(3/2) + C = (2/3)(9‚ąíx¬≤)^(3/2) + C.
questionBank.notesSnippet: For ‚ąęf(g(x))¬∑g'(x)dx, let u = g(x). Here g(x) = 9‚ąíx¬≤, g'(x) = ‚ąí2x. The integral becomes ‚ąę‚ąöu du.
questionBank.timeTip: ‚ąęu^(1/2) du = u^(3/2)/(3/2) = (2/3)u^(3/2). Remember: dividing by 3/2 is the same as multiplying by 2/3.

questionBank.question: Evaluate ‚ąę‚āĀ¬≥ 2x¬∑e^(x¬≤) dx.
questionBank.explanation: Let u = x¬≤, du = 2x dx. Change bounds: x=1‚Üíu=1, x=3‚Üíu=9. ‚ąę‚āĀ‚ĀĻ eŠĶė du = [eŠĶė]‚āĀ‚ĀĻ = e‚ĀĻ ‚ąí e¬Ļ = e‚ĀĻ ‚ąí e.
questionBank.notesSnippet: When changing bounds: substitute x values into u = g(x). x=1 ‚Üí u=1¬≤=1. x=3 ‚Üí u=3¬≤=9. Then integrate in u from 1 to 9.
questionBank.timeTip: Changing bounds is faster than back-substituting. After finding u, immediately compute new bounds. Write u=... next to the new bounds so you don't forget.

questionBank.question: Find ‚ąę x¬≤/(1‚ąíx¬≥)¬≤ dx.
questionBank.explanation: Let u = 1‚ąíx¬≥, du = ‚ąí3x¬≤ dx, so x¬≤ dx = ‚ąídu/3. Integral = ‚ąę(1/u¬≤)(‚ąídu/3) = ‚ąí(1/3)‚ąęu‚ĀĽ¬≤ du = ‚ąí(1/3)(‚ąí1/u) + C = 1/(3u) + C = 1/(3(1‚ąíx¬≥)) + C.
questionBank.notesSnippet: When du doesn't match exactly, solve for dx: x¬≤ dx = ‚ąídu/3. Substitute everything ‚ÄĒ no x terms should remain after substitution.
questionBank.timeTip: If du doesn't perfectly match, solve algebraically. Here x¬≤dx = ‚ąídu/3. The key: after substituting, NO x terms should remain ‚ÄĒ if they do, your u was wrong.

questionBank.question: Find ‚ąę cos(3x) dx.
questionBank.explanation: Let u = 3x, du = 3 dx, so dx = du/3. ‚ąęcos(u)(du/3) = (1/3)sin(u) + C = sin(3x)/3 + C.
questionBank.notesSnippet: For ‚ąęcos(ax)dx, let u = ax, du = a¬∑dx. Result: sin(ax)/a + C. The constant a in the denominator comes from solving for dx = du/a.
questionBank.timeTip: Quick pattern: ‚ąęcos(ax)dx = sin(ax)/a + C. The chain rule constant "a" goes in the denominator. Don't forget to divide by it!

questionBank.question: Evaluate ‚ąę (x¬≤ + 3)/(x + 1) dx. Which is the first step?

choices.B: Perform polynomial long division since degree of numerator ‚Č• degree of denominator

questionBank.explanation: Since degree of numerator (2) ‚Č• degree of denominator (1), we must perform polynomial long division first. Dividing: (x¬≤+3) √∑ (x+1) = x‚ąí1 + 4/(x+1).
questionBank.notesSnippet: When degree(numerator) ‚Č• degree(denominator), use long division first. The result will be a polynomial plus a proper fraction.
questionBank.timeTip: Quick check: compare degrees. If top degree ‚Č• bottom degree ‚Üí divide first. This is non-negotiable ‚ÄĒ you can't integrate directly.

questionBank.question: After performing long division, (x¬≤ + 3)/(x+1) = x ‚ąí 1 + 4/(x+1). Find ‚ąę(x¬≤+3)/(x+1) dx.
questionBank.notesSnippet: After dividing: ‚ąępolynomial dx + ‚ąę(remainder/divisor)dx. The second part usually gives a ln term: ‚ąęk/(x+a)dx = k¬∑ln|x+a| + C.
questionBank.timeTip: After long division, integrate term by term. Polynomial terms use power rule; the remainder fraction uses ‚ąę1/(x+a)dx = ln|x+a|+C.

questionBank.question: Which substitution helps evaluate ‚ąę 1/(x¬≤ + 4x + 5) dx?

choices.B: Complete the square: x¬≤+4x+5 = (x+2)¬≤+1, then let u = x+2

questionBank.explanation: Complete the square: x¬≤+4x+5 = (x+2)¬≤+1. Then ‚ąę1/((x+2)¬≤+1)dx. Let u=x+2, du=dx: ‚ąę1/(u¬≤+1)du = arctan(u)+C = arctan(x+2)+C.
questionBank.notesSnippet: When you have a quadratic in the denominator, complete the square to get the form (x+a)¬≤+b¬≤, which integrates to (1/b)arctan((x+a)/b)+C.
questionBank.timeTip: Completing the square: take half the x-coefficient (here 4/2=2), square it (4), rewrite as (x+2)¬≤+(5‚ąí4) = (x+2)¬≤+1. This sets up arctan.

frqBank.title: FTC and Accumulation Functions
frqBank.context: Let f be a differentiable function defined on the closed interval [0, 7]. The graph of f', the derivative of f, consists of line segments and has the following areas between f' and the x-axis: Region A (above x-axis, 0 ‚Č§ x ‚Č§ 1): area = 2. Region B (below x-axis, 1 ‚Č§ x ‚Č§ 4): area = 7. Region C (above x-axis, 4 ‚Č§ x ‚Č§ 6): area = 3. Region D (above x-axis, 6 ‚Č§ x ‚Č§ 7): area = 1. It is given that f(4) = 10.

parts.label: (a)
parts.question: Find ‚ąę‚āÄ‚Ā∑ f'(x) dx. Show your reasoning using the areas given.
parts.answer: ‚ąę‚āÄ‚Ā∑ f'(x) dx = (net signed area from 0 to 7)
steps: The integral of f' equals the net signed area between f' and the x-axis.
parts.scoring: 1 pt: Correct use of signed areas. 1 pt: Correct signs for each region. 1 pt: Correct answer ‚ąí1.

parts.label: (b)
parts.question: Write an expression for f(x) that involves a definite integral. Use this expression and the information given to find the absolute minimum and absolute maximum values of f on [0, 7]. Justify your answers.
parts.answer: f(x) = f(4) + ‚ąę‚āĄň£ f'(t) dt = 10 + ‚ąę‚āĄň£ f'(t) dt
steps: By the Net Change Theorem: f(x) = f(4) + ‚ąę‚āĄň£ f'(t) dt
f'(x) changes sign at x = 1 and x = 4 (critical points within [0,7])
f(1) = f(0) + 2 = 15 + 2 = 17 (local max, f' changes + to ‚ąí)
f(4) = 10 (given, local min since f' changes ‚ąí to +)
Absolute maximum: f(1) = 17. Absolute minimum: f(4) = 10.
parts.finalAnswer: Absolute max = 17 at x = 1. Absolute min = 10 at x = 4.
parts.scoring: 1 pt: Correct integral expression. 1 pt: Correct critical points identified. 1 pt: Correct max value with justification. 1 pt: Correct min value with justification.

parts.label: (c)
parts.question: Let g be the function defined by g(x) = 5 ‚ąí x¬≤. Find ‚ąę‚āĀ¬≤ x¬∑f'(g(x)) dx.
parts.answer: Use u-substitution with u = g(x) = 5 ‚ąí x¬≤
steps: Let u = g(x) = 5 ‚ąí x¬≤, so du = ‚ąí2x dx, meaning x dx = ‚ąídu/2
Change bounds: when x = 1, u = 5 ‚ąí 1 = 4; when x = 2, u = 5 ‚ąí 4 = 1
‚ąę‚āĀ‚Āī f'(u) du = net area from 1 to 4 = ‚ąíArea B = ‚ąí7
parts.scoring: 1 pt: Correct u-substitution setup. 1 pt: Correct change of bounds. 1 pt: Correct final answer ‚ąí7/2.

frqBank.title: U-Substitution with Definite Integrals
frqBank.context: Consider the functions f(x) = x¬∑cos(x¬≤) and g(x) = 2x/(x¬≤+1). Let R be the region bounded by f and the x-axis on [0, ‚ąöŌÄ]. Let S be the region under g on [0, 2].

parts.label: (a)
parts.question: Find ‚ąę‚āÄ^‚ąöŌÄ x¬∑cos(x¬≤) dx using u-substitution. Show all work.
steps: Let u = x¬≤, so du = 2x dx, meaning x dx = du/2
Change bounds: x = 0 ‚Üí u = 0; x = ‚ąöŌÄ ‚Üí u = ŌÄ
parts.scoring: 1 pt: Correct u and du. 1 pt: Correct new bounds. 1 pt: Correct evaluation.

parts.label: (b)
parts.question: Find ‚ąę‚āÄ¬≤ 2x/(x¬≤+1) dx. Show all work.
steps: Let u = x¬≤ + 1, so du = 2x dx
Change bounds: x = 0 ‚Üí u = 1; x = 2 ‚Üí u = 5
parts.scoring: 1 pt: Correct u-sub setup. 1 pt: Correct new bounds. 1 pt: Correct answer ln(5).

parts.label: (c)
parts.question: Find ‚ąę x¬≥(x‚Āī+3)¬≤ dx (indefinite integral). Show all work and include +C.
steps: Let u = x‚Āī + 3, so du = 4x¬≥ dx, meaning x¬≥ dx = du/4
parts.scoring: 1 pt: Correct u and du. 1 pt: Correct integration. 1 pt: Back-substituted with +C.

frqBank.title: Area Between Curves
frqBank.context: Let f(x) = 6x ‚ąí x¬≤ and g(x) = x¬≤. The graphs of f and g intersect at x = 0 and x = 3.

parts.label: (a)
parts.question: Verify that f(x) ‚Č• g(x) on [0, 3] and find the x-values where the graphs intersect.
parts.answer: Set equal and solve: 6x ‚ąí x¬≤ = x¬≤
steps: Test x = 1: f(1) = 6‚ąí1 = 5, g(1) = 1. Since 5 > 1, f ‚Č• g on [0,3] ‚úď
parts.finalAnswer: Intersections at x = 0 and x = 3. f(x) ‚Č• g(x) on [0, 3].
parts.scoring: 1 pt: Correct intersection points. 1 pt: Confirmed which is on top with evidence.

parts.label: (b)
parts.question: Find the area of the region enclosed between the graphs of f and g.
parts.scoring: 1 pt: Correct integrand (top ‚ąí bottom). 1 pt: Correct antiderivative. 1 pt: Correct answer 9.

parts.label: (c)
parts.question: The region is the base of a solid. Cross sections perpendicular to the x-axis are squares. Find the volume of the solid.
steps: Side length of square = f(x) ‚ąí g(x) = 6x ‚ąí 2x¬≤
parts.scoring: 1 pt: Correct area formula A = s¬≤. 1 pt: Correct integrand. 1 pt: Correct antiderivative. 1 pt: Answer 162/5.

frqBank.title: Particle Motion
frqBank.context: A particle moves along the x-axis. For 0 ‚Č§ t ‚Č§ 6, the velocity of the particle is given by v(t) = t¬≤ ‚ąí 5t + 4. The position of the particle at time t = 0 is x(0) = 2.

parts.label: (a)
parts.question: Find all times t on (0, 6) when the particle changes direction. Justify your answer.
parts.answer: Find when v(t) = 0 and check sign change
steps: Sign of v: v(0.5) = 0.25‚ąí2.5+4 = 1.75 > 0 (positive before t=1)
Particle changes direction at t = 1 (+ to ‚ąí) and t = 4 (‚ąí to +).
parts.finalAnswer: Particle changes direction at t = 1 and t = 4.
parts.scoring: 1 pt: Correct t values. 1 pt: Sign analysis justifying direction change.

parts.label: (b)
parts.question: Find the total distance traveled by the particle from t = 0 to t = 6.
parts.scoring: 1 pt: Correct split at t=1 and t=4. 1 pt: Correct integrals. 1 pt: Correct total = 11.

parts.label: (c)
parts.question: Find the position x(3) of the particle at time t = 3.
parts.scoring: 1 pt: Correct Net Change Theorem setup. 1 pt: Correct answer 1/2.

frqBank.title: Riemann Sums and Definite Integrals
frqBank.context:
The function h is continuous on the closed interval [1, 13]. Selected values of h are given in the table below:
x:    1    4    6    10   13
h(x): 3    7    2     8    5

parts.label: (a)
parts.question: Approximate ‚ąę‚āĀ¬Ļ¬≥ h(x)dx using a left Riemann sum with 4 subintervals indicated by the table. Show your work.
parts.answer: Left Riemann sum using left endpoints of each subinterval
parts.scoring: 1 pt: Correct subintervals and widths. 1 pt: Left endpoint values. 1 pt: Correct sum = 55.

parts.label: (b)
parts.question: Approximate ‚ąę‚āĀ¬Ļ¬≥ h(x)dx using a trapezoidal sum with the same 4 subintervals. Show your work.
parts.answer: Trapezoid rule: (őĒx/2)[h(left) + h(right)] for each subinterval
parts.scoring: 1 pt: Correct trapezoidal formula. 1 pt: Correct individual trapezoids. 1 pt: Correct total 63.5.

parts.label: (c)
parts.question: If h is known to be concave down on [1, 13], is the trapezoidal sum an overestimate or underestimate of ‚ąę‚āĀ¬Ļ¬≥ h(x)dx? Justify your answer.
steps: When a function is concave down, the trapezoids lie BELOW the curve.
The chord connecting two points on a concave-down curve falls below the arc.
Therefore, the trapezoidal sum gives an underestimate of the actual integral.
parts.finalAnswer: Underestimate ‚ÄĒ for concave down functions, the trapezoidal rule lies below the curve.
parts.scoring: 1 pt: Correct answer (underestimate). 1 pt: Correct justification referencing concavity.

frqBank.title: Mixed Applications ‚ÄĒ Area, Volume, Average Value
frqBank.context: Let f(x) = 3sin(ŌÄx/2) and g(x) = x on the interval [0, 2].

parts.label: (a)
parts.question: Find the average value of f(x) = 3sin(ŌÄx/2) on [0, 2].
steps: Let u = ŌÄx/2, du = ŌÄ/2 dx, so dx = 2du/ŌÄ
parts.scoring: 1 pt: Correct average value formula. 1 pt: Correct antiderivative. 1 pt: Answer 6/ŌÄ.

parts.label: (b)
parts.question: Find the area of the region between f(x) = 3sin(ŌÄx/2) and g(x) = x on [0, 2]. (Use calculator as needed.)
steps: 3sin(ŌÄx/2) = x intersects at x ‚Čą 0 and x ‚Čą 1.762 (calculator)
parts.scoring: 1 pt: Correct intersection found. 1 pt: Correct setup with split. 1 pt: Correct area.

parts.label: (c)
parts.question: The region between f and the x-axis on [0, 2] is revolved around the x-axis. Find the volume using the disc method.
parts.scoring: 1 pt: Correct disc formula V=ŌÄ‚ąęr¬≤dx. 1 pt: Correct trig identity used. 1 pt: Correct answer 9ŌÄ.

6.1.title: 6-1: Accumulations of Rates of Change
6.1.content:
CHA-4.A ¬∑ Key Concept

What is Accumulation?

The area of the region between the graph of a rate of change function and the x-axis gives the accumulation of change.

Core Idea

If f(t) is a rate of change (velocity, flow rate, etc.), then the area between f(t) and the x-axis from t=a to t=b represents the total change in the quantity over that interval.

  • If the rate is positive over an interval ‚Üí accumulated change is positive
  • If the rate is negative over an interval ‚Üí accumulated change is negative
  • Accumulation can be evaluated geometrically using rectangles, triangles, etc.

Area Under the Curve

The region between a function and the x-axis is called the area under the curve. "Under" does NOT mean below ‚ÄĒ it means between the function and the x-axis.

distance = rate √ó time  ‚Üí  area = rate √ó interval width
AP Note: Real-world context is essential. Units of the accumulation = units of f(t) √ó units of t. (ft/sec √ó sec = ft)

6.2.title: 6-2: Approximating Areas with Riemann Sums
6.2.content:
LIM-5.A ¬∑ Riemann Sums

Methods of Approximation

When we can't compute exact area, we use rectangles (Riemann sums) or trapezoids. You must decide: (1) how many subintervals n, and (2) which endpoint to use for height.

Formula

őĒx = (b ‚ąí a) / n   (width of each rectangle)

Types of Riemann Sums

  • Left Riemann Sum (LRAM): Use left endpoint of each subinterval for height
  • Right Riemann Sum (RRAM): Use right endpoint of each subinterval for height
  • Midpoint Rule (MRAM): Use midpoint of each subinterval for height
  • Trapezoidal Rule: Average of left and right ‚Üí őĒx/2 ¬∑ [f(x‚āÄ) + 2f(x‚āĀ) + ... + f(x‚āô)]

Over vs. Underestimates

  • If f is increasing: Left sum UNDERESTIMATES, Right sum OVERESTIMATES
  • If f is decreasing: Left sum OVERESTIMATES, Right sum UNDERESTIMATES
  • If f is concave up: Trapezoid OVERESTIMATES, Midpoint UNDERESTIMATES
  • If f is concave down: Trapezoid UNDERESTIMATES, Midpoint OVERESTIMATES
AP Note: Overestimate/underestimate questions appear EVERY year. Memorize the relationships above!

6.3.title: 6-3: Riemann Sums, Summation Notation, and Definite Integral Notation
6.3.content:
FUN-5.A ¬∑ Definite Integral Notation

From Riemann Sums to Definite Integrals

As n ‚Üí ‚ąě (infinitely many rectangles), the Riemann sum approaches the exact area ‚ÄĒ this is the definite integral.

lim(n‚Üí‚ąě) ő£ŠĶĘ‚āĆ‚āĀ‚ĀŅ f(xŠĶĘ*)¬∑őĒx = ‚ąę‚āźŠĶá f(x) dx
Notation Breakdown

‚ąę‚āźŠĶá f(x) dx ‚ÄĒ "a" is the lower bound, "b" is the upper bound, f(x) is the integrand, dx indicates the variable of integration

Sigma (Summation) Notation

ő£ŠĶĘ‚āĆ‚āĀ‚ĀŅ f(xŠĶĘ)¬∑őĒx means: add up the areas of n rectangles, where each has height f(xŠĶĘ) and width őĒx.

AP Note: You may be asked to identify what a given sum equals as a definite integral by matching the form lim ő£ f(xŠĶĘ)¬∑őĒx = ‚ąę‚āźŠĶá f(x) dx.

6.4.title: 6-4 & 6-5: The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus and Accumulation Functions
6.4.content:
FUN-5.A ¬∑ THE most important theorem in AP Calc

FTC Part 2 ‚ÄĒ Evaluation Theorem

‚ąę‚āźŠĶá f(x) dx = F(b) ‚ąí F(a)   where F'(x) = f(x)

F(x) is called the antiderivative of f(x). Find F, plug in b, subtract F(a).

AP Note: This is GUARANTEED to be on the AP exam. Think of it as another derivative rule to memorize!

FTC Part 1 ‚ÄĒ Derivative of Accumulation Functions

d/dx [‚ąę‚āźň£ f(t) dt] = f(x)

Derivatives and integrals are inverses of each other ‚ÄĒ they cancel out.

With Chain Rule (Very Common on AP!)

d/dx [‚ąę‚āźŠĶć‚ĀĹň£‚Āĺ f(t) dt] = f(g(x)) ¬∑ g'(x)

  • Replace t with g(x) in the integrand
  • Multiply by the derivative of the upper bound g'(x)
  • If the variable is in the LOWER bound, flip the sign: d/dx[‚ąę‚āďŠĶá f(t)dt] = -f(x)

Accumulation Functions

g(x) = ‚ąę‚āźň£ f(t) dt defines g as a function. Given a graph of f, you can find values of g by computing signed areas geometrically.

AP Note: g'(x) = f(x), so the graph of f tells you where g is increasing (f > 0) and decreasing (f < 0). Max/min of g occur where f changes sign.

6.6.title: 6-6: Properties of Definite Integrals
6.6.content:
FUN-6.A ¬∑ Properties

Key Properties

‚ąę‚āźŠĶÉ f(x) dx = 0   (same bounds ‚Üí zero)
‚ąę‚āźŠĶá f(x) dx = ‚ąí‚ąęŠĶ¶‚āź f(x) dx   (flip bounds ‚Üí flip sign)
‚ąę‚āźŠ∂ú f(x) dx = ‚ąę‚āźŠĶá f(x) dx + ‚ąęŠĶ¶Š∂ú f(x) dx   (split at any point b)
‚ąę‚āźŠĶá k¬∑f(x) dx = k¬∑‚ąę‚āźŠĶá f(x) dx   (constant multiplier)
‚ąę‚āźŠĶá [f(x) ¬Ī g(x)] dx = ‚ąę‚āźŠĶá f(x) dx ¬Ī ‚ąę‚āźŠĶá g(x) dx
AP Note: These properties let you break apart complex integrals. If ‚ąę‚āÄ‚ĀĶ=10 and ‚ąę‚āÄ¬≥=4, then ‚ąę‚āÉ‚ĀĶ = 10‚ąí4 = 6. Practice using given integral values to find others.

6.7.title: 6-7: The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus and Definite Integrals
6.7.content:
FUN-5.A ¬∑ FTC in Action

Using FTC to Evaluate Definite Integrals

To evaluate ‚ąę‚āźŠĶá f(x) dx: find antiderivative F(x), then compute F(b) ‚ąí F(a).

‚ąę‚āĀ¬≥ x¬≤ dx = [x¬≥/3]‚āĀ¬≥ = 27/3 ‚ąí 1/3 = 26/3

The constant of integration C cancels out: F(b)+C ‚ąí (F(a)+C) = F(b) ‚ąí F(a). So we omit C for definite integrals.

AP Note: Use bracket notation [F(x)]‚āźŠĶá to show evaluating at bounds. Always show this step on the AP exam for full credit.

6.8.title: 6-8: Antiderivatives, Indefinite Integrals, and Basic Integration Rules
6.8.content:
FUN-6.D ¬∑ Basic Rules

Power Rule for Integrals

‚ąęx‚ĀŅ dx = x‚ĀŅ‚Āļ¬Ļ/(n+1) + C   (n ‚Č† ‚ąí1)

Add 1 to the exponent, divide by the new exponent. Always add + C for indefinite integrals.

Common Antiderivatives to Memorize

  • ‚ąęeň£ dx = eň£ + C
  • ‚ąę1/x dx = ln|x| + C
  • ‚ąęsin(x) dx = ‚ąícos(x) + C
  • ‚ąęcos(x) dx = sin(x) + C
  • ‚ąęsec¬≤(x) dx = tan(x) + C
  • ‚ąęsec(x)tan(x) dx = sec(x) + C
  • ‚ąę1/‚ąö(1‚ąíx¬≤) dx = arcsin(x) + C
  • ‚ąę1/(1+x¬≤) dx = arctan(x) + C
AP Note: Trig integrals appear CONSTANTLY. The most common mistake is forgetting the negative on ‚ąęsin(x)dx = ‚ąícos(x)+C.

6.9.title: 6-9: Integration with U-Substitution
6.9.content:
FUN-6.D ¬∑ U-Substitution

The U-Substitution Method

Use when you have a composite function ‚ÄĒ a function inside another function. You need the inside function's derivative also present.

Steps

1. Choose u = inner function   2. Find du = u'(x) dx   3. Solve for dx = du/u'(x)   4. Substitute all x terms with u   5. Integrate in terms of u   6. Back-substitute u with original expression

Example

‚ąę 2x¬∑e^(x¬≤) dx: Let u = x¬≤, du = 2x dx ‚Üí ‚ąęeŠĶė du = eŠĶė + C = e^(x¬≤) + C

U-Sub with Definite Integrals

Two methods: (1) Change the bounds: when you substitute u, change a and b to u(a) and u(b). Integrate in u, don't back-substitute. (2) Back-substitute and evaluate with original bounds.

AP Note: Changing bounds is faster on the AP exam and avoids back-substitution errors. Always show the new bounds clearly.

6.10.title: 6-10: Long Division and Completing the Square
6.10.content:
FUN-6.D ¬∑ Advanced Techniques

Long Division for Rational Functions

When the degree of the numerator ‚Č• degree of denominator, use polynomial long division FIRST to simplify, then integrate each term.

‚ąę(x¬≤ + 3)/(x + 1) dx ‚Üí divide first ‚Üí ‚ąę(x ‚ąí 1 + 4/(x+1)) dx

Completing the Square ‚Üí Inverse Trig

For integrals with quadratics in the denominator, complete the square to get the form 1/(u¬≤ + a¬≤), which integrates to (1/a)arctan(u/a) + C.

‚ąę1/(x¬≤ + 4x + 5) dx ‚Üí complete square ‚Üí ‚ąę1/((x+2)¬≤ + 1) dx = arctan(x+2) + C
AP Note: Long division appears on the AP exam. Always check: is degree of numerator ‚Č• denominator? If yes, divide first.

Unit 6 ‚ÄĒ Materials on this site include 9 notes PDFs, 18 homework PDFs, and 4 review PDFs. Open Materials (PDFs) in the sidebar for the full list.

The formula blocks below are the shared AP Calc AB reference; your unit packets explain how they show up in your course.
